Signals in Linux  
Asynchronous notifications delivered by the  
kernel to processes

Signal  
Signals are software interrupts delivered to a process  
• Signals interrupt normal program flow.  
• They notify a process that some event occurred (e.g., segmentation fault, child exit, timer expiry, CTRL+C,  
etc.).  
• Think of signals as asynchronous notifications sent to a process. Async → can arrive anytime  
• #include <signal.h>  
• Each signal in Linux is identified by unique number assigned to them  
• Used for:  
• Process control  
• Exception handling  
Process  
Event  
Kernel  
Signal  
• Inter-process communication  
Handler  
• Reporting asynchronous events  
Signal Handling  
• Kernel can perform one of the 3 option depending upon what prcess has asked  
o Ignore the signal  
o Catch and handle the signal – Kernel will suspend execution of the process's current code and jumps  
to a signal handler and the returns to main  
o Perform default action – if there is no user defined signal handler, then default action assigned to that  
sigal is performed

System Calls - Sending Signals  
Syntax  
Parameters  
Return & Errors  
signal to process group or PID  
pid: target process  
0 on success  
int kill(pid_t pid, int sig)  
sig: signal number  
-1 on error (ESRCH, EPERM)  
Sends signal to itself  
sig: signal number  
0 or -1  
int raise(int sig)  
signal to specific thread id  
For threads  
0 or -1  
int tgkill(tgid, tid, sig)  
System Calls - Installing Signal Handlers  
Syntax  
Parameters  
Return & Errors  
sig: signal  
sighandler_t signal(int sig, sighandler_t func)  
Old handler or SIG_ERR  
func: handler function  
int sigaction(int sig,  
act: new action  
const struct sigaction *act,  
struct sigaction *old)  
0 or -1  
old: store previous  
Catching ctrl+c Signal  
#include <signal.h>  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
• SIGINT comes from terminal  
driver when user presses Ctrl+C.  
void handler(int sig) {  
printf("Caught signal %d\n", sig);  
}
int main() {  
signal(SIGINT, handler); // Catch Ctrl+C  
while (1) {  
printf("Running...\n");  
• Better: use write() inside  
handler  
• Never call non-reentrant  
functions from handlers in  
production code.  
sleep(1);  
}
return 0;  
}
Catching Alarm Signal  
#include <stdio.h>  
• alarm() schedules SIGALRM after given  
seconds.  
#include <signal.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
• pause() is blocked until next signal → good  
for demo.  
void display_message(int s) {  
printf("Generated SIGALARM\n" );  
alarm(2); //for every second  
}
int main(void) {  
signal(SIGALRM, display_message);  
alarm(2);  
while (1)  
pause(); // sleep until a signal arrives;  
}
User-Defined Signals  
Signal  
Number  
Typically 10  
Typically 12  
Default Action  
Terminate  
Usage  
SIGUSR1  
SIGUSR2  
Application-defined event  
Application-defined event  
Terminate  
SIGUSR1 / SIGUSR2 are general-purpose signals.  
User Signal: Parent Receiving Signal from Child  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <stdlib.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <signal.h>  
#include <sys/types.h>  
if (pid < 0) {perror("Fork failed"); exit(1);}  
if (pid == 0) { // Child process  
sleep(2); // Simulate some work  
kill(getppid(), SIGUSR1); // Send signal to  
parent  
void handle_signal(int sig) {  
exit(0);  
printf("Parent received signal: %d from child\n",  
} else { // Parent process  
sig);  
}
printf("Parent waiting for signal from  
child...\n");  
pause(); // Wait for signal  
int main() {  
wait();  
signal(SIGUSR1, handle_signal);  
// Register signal handler  
pid_t pid = fork();  
printf("Parent exiting.\n");  
}
return 0;  
}
Child  
SIGUSR1  
Parent  
Scenario: Log Monitoring System Using SIGUSR1  
Between Unrelated Processes  
Context:  
You are building a simple software setup with two  
independent processes:  
Worker Process: Continuously generates log  
entries in a file app.log.  
Monitor Process: Periodically checks the size of  
app.log. When the file grows beyond a limit (given  
user), the Monitor sends SIGUSR1 to the Worker.  
by  
When the Worker receives SIGUSR1, it must switch from  
detailed logging to minimal logging.  
Monitor  
SIGUSR1  
Worker  
PART 1 — Implement the Worker Process  
Create a C file named worker.c  
• Print own PID on startup  
• Open a file app.log in append mode  
• Install a signal handler for SIGUSR1  
• Maintain a global flag minimal_mode = 0  
• Inside the handler, set minimal_mode = 1  
• Keep writing logs every 1 second:  
• If minimal_mode == 0: write “detailed log entry…”  
• Else: write “minimal log entry”  
PART 2 — Implement the Monitor Process  
Create a C file named monitor.c  
• Take two command-line arguments:  
• PID of worker  
• Maximum allowed file size in bytes  
• Every 2 seconds:  
• Check size of app.log using stat()  
• If file size > limit → print a message and send SIGUSR1 to worker using kill(pid, SIGUSR1)  
Async-Signal-Safe Functions -  
What you can safely call in a handler  
Handlers run asynchronously—call only async-signal-safe functions.c  
• A signal handler can interrupt code anytime → avoid non-reentrant functions.  
• Only call async-signal-safe functions (e.g., write, _exit, sigatomic-based flag set).  
• Avoid: printf, malloc/free, pthread_mutex_*, std::iostream, most library calls.  
• Use a global volatile sig_atomic_t flag to communicate from handler → main loop checks it.  
• For printing/logging: buffer in main; handler only sets a flag and returns fast.  
Common safe examples:  
_exit, write, signal(with caveats), sigaction, sigaddset/sigemptyset, kill, alarm, pause, sleep  
Blocking & Masking Signals  
Temporarily block signals to protect critical sections.  
• Blocking ≠ ignoring: delivery is deferred while blocked; pending until unblocked.  
• Typical pattern:  
sigemptyset(&set); sigaddset(&set, SIGINT);  
sigprocmask(SIG_BLOCK, &set, &old); // enter critical section  
critical section  
sigprocmask(SIG_SETMASK, &old, NULL); // exit critical  
• Handlers + masks: install with sigaction; you can block specific signals during a handler via sa_mask.  
#include <signal.h>  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
int main(void) {  
sigset_t set, old; sigemptyset(&set); sigaddset(&set, SIGINT);  
// Block SIGINT for 5 seconds (simulate critical section)  
sigprocmask(SIG_BLOCK, &set, &old);  
puts("Critical section: try Ctrl+C now (will be pending)...");  
sleep(5);  
sigset_t pend; sigpending(&pend);  
if (sigismember(&pend, SIGINT)) puts("SIGINT is pending");  
// Unblock (delivery happens here if pending)  
sigprocmask(SIG_SETMASK, &old, NULL);  
puts("Unblocked. If you pressed Ctrl+C, SIGINT should deliver now.");  
pause(); // wait for any signal to observe delivery  
}
Real-Time Signals (SIGRTMIN … SIGRTMAX)  
• Range: SIGRTMIN … SIGRTMAX (implementation-defined count; typically 32).  
• Queued: multiple occurrences are queued (not coalesced).  
• Priority: higher numbers typically have higher delivery priority.  
• Payload: use sigqueue() to send with an integer/ptr (union sigval).  
• Install with sigaction + SA_SIGINFO to read siginfo_t (sender pid, value, etc.).  
• Great for: event counts, lightweight notifications between processes/threads.  
• Caution: Still subject to async-signal rules; keep handlers minimal.  
pid_t pid = atoi(argv[1]);  
union sigval v; v.sival_int = 42;  
sigqueue(pid, SIGRTMIN, v);  
return 0;  
// payload  
}
// Receiver  
#include <signal.h>  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
static void rt_handler(int sig, siginfo_t *si, void *ctx) {  
(void)ctx;  
// Only async-safe printing is write(); here we just show concept for class:  
printf("Got RT signal %d with value=%d from pid=%d\n",  
sig, si->si_value.sival_int, si->si_pid);  
}
int main(void) {  
struct sigaction sa = {0};  
sa.sa_sigaction = rt_handler;  
sa.sa_flags = SA_SIGINFO;  
sigaction(SIGRTMIN, &sa, NULL);  
signal() vs sigaction()  
• signal() — legacy, inconsistent semantics across systems; limited control.  
• sigaction() — modern, reliable, supports SA_SIGINFO, masks, flags (SA_RESTART, etc.).  
• Rule of thumb: use sigaction() in all new code.
